original_abstract: The political landscape in Slovakia has been significantly reshaped by the ascent of new parties leveraging anti-corruption, anti-establishment, and populist rhetoric. This shift has disrupted the traditional alternation between right-wing and left-wing blocs; however, the inherent heterogeneity of these new actors has not prevented the successful formation of governing coalitions or the ratification of formal agreements. In fact, Slovak governance has seen a stabilization of coalition patterns that increasingly align with the "ministerial government" model. These administrations rely on comprehensive, public coalition agreements that explicitly dictate office allocation, manage inter-party relations, and utilize a "coalition council" to resolve internal disputes. Interestingly, this institutional stabilization remained resilient through the global financial and migration crises—factors that had minimal impact on Slovakia’s democratic quality. Conversely, the COVID-19 pandemic served as a critical turning point, facilitating a temporary surge in prime ministerial dominance that disrupted the gradual consolidation of a more collaborative coalition governance model.
humanized_abstract: Slovakia’s politics has undergone a profound change as new anti-corruption, anti-elitist and populist parties emerged to power under the banner of combating corruption, the political elite, as well as by promising a change in politics, or by populist or even Eurosceptic rhetoric, hence breaking the pattern of alternating right and left coalition formations. While internally very diverse, the new political actors were able to establish effective governments and even formally agree to coalitions. Thus, Slovak coalition governance continued to exhibit a high degree of stability at the ministerial level and along the lines of the model of a ‘ministerial government’, based on a detailed, publicly available contract defining the distribution of ministers’ portfolios, rules of interaction among coalition members, and an associated ‘coalition council’ tasked with resolving potential disputes. This pattern proved to be robust even in the face of the challenges presented by the financial crisis and the migration crisis, which – as in the other CEE countries – had no considerable negative impact on democratic quality. It was only the COVID-19 crisis that provided an opportunity for Prime Ministers to strengthen their position in relation to the other coalition parties and to a degree to undermine the achievements of the recent development of more coalition-friendly governance in Slovakia.
